question: What does the nuclear binding energy curve reveal?

options: Binding energy per nucleon rises steeply for light nuclei, peaks near $^{56}$Fe/$^{62}$Ni at ~8.8 MeV/nucleon, then slowly declines for heavy nuclei; fusion of light nuclei and fission of heavy nuclei both release energy by moving toward the peak
Binding energy per nucleon increases without limit with mass number (this describes a different phenomenon) (this conflates cause and effect)
All nuclei have the same binding energy per nucleon (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
Binding energy is largest for the lightest nuclei (hydrogen) (a common misconception) (not supported by the evidence) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: The binding energy $B = [Zm_p + Nm_n - M(Z,N)]c^2$ measures the mass defect holding a nucleus together. Per-nucleon, it climbs from deuterium to a broad maximum around iron-56/nickel-62, then gently falls. This single curve explains stellar energy: light nuclei release energy by fusing (up to iron), heavy nuclei by fissioning (down to the iron region). It also explains why iron-peak elements are cosmically abundant and why no energy can be extracted by fusing beyond iron. The semi-empirical mass formula models its shape via volume, surface, Coulomb, asymmetry, and pairing terms.

active.prompt: Match each term of the semi-empirical (liquid-drop) mass formula:

pairs.definition: Bulk binding; each nucleon bonds to neighbours (saturating strong force) $\propto A$
Surface nucleons have fewer neighbours; reduces binding like surface tension
Proton-proton electrostatic repulsion; grows with charge, limits heavy nuclei
$-a_A(N-Z)^2/A$ favours $N\approx Z$; pairing $\delta$ favours even-even nuclei

active.explanation: The Bethe-Weizsacker (liquid-drop) formula treats the nucleus as an incompressible charged droplet. It reproduces the binding-energy curve, predicts the valley of stability ($N \approx Z$ for light, neutron-rich for heavy), the onset of fission instability, and (with the pairing term) the dominance of even-even stable nuclei. Deviations at magic numbers signal shell structure beyond the droplet picture.

question: What holds the nucleus together against proton-proton repulsion?

options: The residual strong nuclear force — short-range (~1-2 fm), strongly attractive, charge-independent — binds nucleons; it is the leftover of the colour force between quarks, mediated at the nuclear scale by pion (meson) exchange (Yukawa)
Gravity between nucleons (not supported by the evidence) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this conflates cause and effect)
The electromagnetic force, which is attractive between protons (true only as a special case, not in general) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
The weak force binds the nucleus (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (a common misconception) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
passive.explanation: Protons repel electrostatically, so a stronger, shorter-range attraction is required. The residual strong force (nuclear force) is the colour interaction's 'spillover' between colour-neutral nucleons, analogous to the van der Waals force between neutral atoms. Yukawa (1935) modelled it as pion exchange, predicting a force range $\sim \hbar/m_\pi c \approx 1.4$ fm — the pion was found in 1947. The force is attractive at ~1 fm but strongly repulsive at very short range (hard core), giving nuclear saturation (constant density, $B/A$ roughly constant). It is charge-independent (nearly the same for nn, pp, np), reflecting isospin symmetry.

question: What are nuclear magic numbers and what do they signify?

options: $N$ or $Z$ = 2, 8, 20, 28, 50, 82, 126: closed nucleon shells giving extra stability (higher binding, larger separation energies, spherical shape, low excitation); analogous to noble-gas electron shells, explained by the nuclear shell model with strong spin-orbit coupling
Numbers at which nuclei become radioactive (not supported by the evidence) (this describes a different phenomenon) (true only as a special case, not in general)
Random numbers with no physical meaning (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
Numbers where nuclei are least bound (this conflates cause and effect) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
passive.explanation: Doubly-magic nuclei ($^4$He, $^{16}$O, $^{40}$Ca, $^{48}$Ca, $^{208}$Pb) are exceptionally stable and spherical. The magic numbers were a puzzle for the liquid-drop model but emerge from the shell model (Goeppert-Mayer, Jensen, Nobel 1963) once a strong spin-orbit coupling is included, which reorders single-particle levels to produce the observed gaps. Evidence: peaks in binding energy, abundance, and neutron-capture cross-section dips, high first-excited-state energies. Near the drip lines the traditional magic numbers can shift (e.g. new magic numbers $N=16$, disappearance of $N=20$ in exotic nuclei) — an active research frontier.

question: How does nuclear size scale with mass number?

options: Nuclear radius $R \approx r_0 A^{1/3}$ with $r_0 \approx 1.2$ fm, so nuclear volume $\propto A$ and density is roughly constant ($\sim 0.16$ nucleons/fm$^3$) — nuclear matter is nearly incompressible, like a liquid drop
$R \propto A$, so density falls with mass (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon) (true only as a special case, not in general)
$R \propto A^{2/3}$, a surface-dominated scaling (this conflates cause and effect) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
Nuclear size is independent of $A$ (an outdated view superseded by later work) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: Electron-scattering (Hofstadter), muonic-atom, and other measurements give $R \approx 1.2\,A^{1/3}$ fm. The $A^{1/3}$ law means volume $\propto A$, hence constant central density — the saturation property of the strong force (each nucleon interacts only with near neighbours, like molecules in a liquid). The charge density is roughly flat in the interior and falls off over a ~2.4 fm surface skin (Woods-Saxon / Fermi distribution). This near-constant density underlies the liquid-drop model and the concept of 'nuclear matter' relevant to neutron stars. The proton charge radius itself ($\sim 0.84$ fm) was the subject of the recent 'proton radius puzzle'.

question: What is isospin symmetry in nuclear physics?

options: Treating proton and neutron as two states of one 'nucleon' under an approximate $SU(2)$ symmetry (isospin); the strong force is nearly charge-independent, so isospin multiplets (e.g. mirror nuclei) have closely matching level schemes, broken mainly by the Coulomb force and the small $u$-$d$ mass difference
The symmetry between electrons and neutrinos (a common misconception) (not supported by the evidence) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this conflates cause and effect)
An exact symmetry never broken in nature (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
A symmetry only of the electromagnetic interaction (true only as a special case, not in general) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
passive.explanation: Heisenberg (1932) noted the proton and neutron have nearly equal masses and identical strong interactions, suggesting an internal $SU(2)$ doublet with isospin $T = 1/2$ ($T_3 = +1/2$ proton, $-1/2$ neutron). Nuclear states organise into isospin multiplets; mirror nuclei (swap $N\leftrightarrow Z$, e.g. $^{13}$C/$^{13}$N) show nearly identical excitation spectra after correcting for Coulomb energy — strong evidence. Isospin is broken by electromagnetism and the up-down quark mass difference, but remains a powerful classification and selection-rule tool. It is the nuclear-scale shadow of the flavour $SU(2)$ symmetry of light quarks in QCD, and generalises to $SU(3)$ flavour for strange hadrons.

question: What is the neutron/proton drip line?

options: The boundary in the nuclear chart beyond which a nucleus is unbound to nucleon emission (the separation energy goes negative): adding one more neutron (neutron drip line) or proton (proton drip line) makes it immediately 'drip' out; it delimits the existence of bound nuclei
The line of stable nuclei (this describes a different phenomenon) (a common misconception) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
The line where alpha decay begins (not supported by the evidence) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this conflates cause and effect) (true only as a special case, not in general)
A line marking the most tightly bound nuclei (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this describes a different phenomenon)
passive.explanation: As one adds neutrons (or protons) to a fixed element, the last-nucleon separation energy decreases until it reaches zero — beyond that the extra nucleon is unbound and the nucleus cannot exist as a bound state. The proton drip line is fairly well mapped (Coulomb barrier helps localise it); the neutron drip line is known experimentally only up to neon-ish and predicted to lie far from stability for heavier elements, the target of rare-isotope facilities (FRIB, RIBF). Near the drip lines exotic phenomena appear: halo nuclei (e.g. $^{11}$Li), changed magic numbers, two-nucleon emission, and the path of the astrophysical r-process. Mapping the drip lines defines the very limits of nuclear existence and tests nuclear forces in extreme isospin.
